does: This paper proposes HalluScope, a benchmark for systematically analyzing hallucination sources in large vision-language models (LVLMs), and finds that hallucinations primarily stem from excessive reliance on textual priors and background knowledge embedded in prompts rather than limitations of the vision backbone. The authors then introduce HalluVL-DPO, a fine-tuning framework that uses preference optimization with a curated training dataset to guide LVLMs toward visually grounded responses by teaching models to prefer factually correct outputs over hallucinated ones based on actual image content.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition of hallucinations as a critical failure mode in LVLMs, extending prior research that attributed hallucinations to vision backbone limitations or language model dominance by isolating the specific role of textual instruction priors. It connects to broader literature on vision-language alignment and grounding, while incorporating recent advances in preference optimization techniques (such as DPO) from the language model safety community to address multimodal systems, and draws on methodologies for constructing preference-based training datasets.
enables: This research enables more targeted interventions for reducing hallucinations in vision-language systems by identifying textual priors as the primary culprit, opening pathways for developing prompt engineering strategies that minimize hallucination-inducing instructions. It provides a benchmark infrastructure for future work evaluating hallucination mitigation techniques and suggests that preference optimization frameworks can be adapted effectively to multimodal settings, potentially inspiring similar fine-tuning approaches for other vision-language tasks where grounding to visual content is critical.
